EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area in southeast South Dakota with a warm front extending to the southeast pushed north and brought numerous severe thunderstorms to parts of central and northeast South Dakota.  Large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding, along with several tornadoes occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down east of Stephan damaging two homes. The main home at the residence had part of its metal roof taken off, while a secondary home lost its entire roof structure. A wooden barn had its main door blown in resulting in a lifting of the roof structure. This allowed the exterior walls to collapse with the roof then settling back down on the debris. A metal machine shed was destroyed along with a semi-tractor trailer blown over on its side. As the tornado continued north, a tree grove suffered extensive damage with numerous trees being topped and large branches broken off.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed in Buffalo county and grew in size to approximately a quarter mile wide as it tracked northeast in open country. The tornado tracked two miles before dissipating near the Hand county line. The tornado did hit a shelter belt causing tree damage with several softwood trees uprooted along with large branches broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning thunderstorm brought a lightning strike to the Citizen's Building on South Main Street in Aberdeen. The lightning strike took out the elevator and phone system on the sixth floor with power disruptions elsewhere.  The fire department was called to the building when a burning smell was noticed with no fire found. A person watched the lightning strike the lightning rod on the top of the building.
An isolated thunderstorm produced quarter size hail near Gary in Deuel County.
Very strong northwest winds, sustained above 40 mph at times with gusts above 60 mph, developed in the late morning of January 16th, and persisted into the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong northwest winds were occasionally over 40 mph sustained, with gusts near 60 mph. A gust to 59 mph was recorded at Gregory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta Clipper system generated light snow and strong winds across the region resulting in blizzard conditions. On Sunday morning, a band of moderate to heavy snow showers developed over North Dakota and swept down through our region producing cloud to ground lightning and thundersnow at times.  Snowfall amounts were generally three inches or less.  Wind gusts ranged from 45 to 55 mph at times.  Several no travel advisories were issued due to poor visibilities in blizzard conditions with state officials closing a large portion of Interstate-29 from Brookings to the North Dakota border.  With low visibilities near mile marker 178 on Interstate-29 near Watertown, a pickup struck the back of a car on Sunday afternoon of the 26th.  Two people were injured with a teenager dying a few days later as result of her injuries.

The combination of low temperatures in the teens below zero and northwesterly winds of 10 to 15 mph produced bitter cold wind chills of 35 below to 40 degrees below zero during the early morning hours on the 23rd. Winds diminished through the morning hours with wind chills improving before noon.

The combination of low temperatures in the teens below zero and northwesterly winds of 10 to 15 mph produced bitter cold wind chills of 35 below to 40 degrees below zero during the early morning hours on the 23rd. Winds diminished through the morning hours with wind chills improving before noon.

A clipper system passing across the region brought light snowfall and very strong northwest winds gusting in excess of 70 mph at times resulting in blizzard conditions.  The highest wind gusts include; 76 mph at the Brown County Landfill; 69 mph in Aberdeen and Cravens Corner; 52 mph near Webster; and 52 mph in Sisseton. Blizzard conditions ended during the late afternoon hours. A no travel advisory was issued in Grant, Codington, Hamlin, and Spink Counties due to poor visibilities.  The blizzard led to the cancellation of several area activities and schools and nearly impossible travel conditions.

A strong area of surface high pressure building into the region behind an Arctic cold front brought high winds to central and north central South Dakota during the early morning hours of the 16th.  Some of the strongest wind gusts include; 69 mph near Whitlocks Bay; 68 mph near Bullhead; 67 mph near Trail City; 66 mph in Foster Bay; 65 mph near Mellette and in Presho; 64 mph near Harrold and in Murdo; and 63 mph in Pierre, Reliance, and Miranda.  The strong winds diminished during the late afternoon hours of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of northwest winds gusting to over 50 mph at times combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall of up to 2 inches to cause blizzard conditions from late morning to the start of the evening on January 16th. The blizzard caused widespread visibilities of less than one quarter mile in open areas. Travel was brought to a temporary halt and schools and some businesses were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to over 50 mph combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall of up to 2 inches to cause widespread frequent visibilities below a quarter mile in blowing snow. Schools and some businesses closed as travel temporarily became difficult to impossible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell during the early morning hours of the 3rd.  By the mid-morning hours, strong southerly winds developed ahead a clipper system.  Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts exceeding 50 mph cause widespread blowing and drifting snow for the Sisseton Hills area.  Travel was significantly affected along Interstate-29 and Highway 12.  The white out conditions contributed to a rollover accident in Day County.

Travel was significantly affected along Interstate-29 and Highway 12.  The white out conditions contributed to a rollover accident in Day County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moving across the region brought light snowfall and very strong northwest winds to the higher terrain areas of northeastern South Dakota.  Snowfall  between a half to one inch along with winds of 30 to 40 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions. Travel along Interstate-29 and Highway 12 was significantly affected.

A strong area of surface high pressure building into the region behind an Arctic cold front brought high winds to central and north central South Dakota during the early morning hours of the 16th.  Some of the strongest wind gusts include; 69 mph near Whitlocks Bay; 68 mph near Bullhead; 67 mph near Trail City; 66 mph in Foster Bay; 65 mph near Mellette and in Presho; 64 mph near Harrold and in Murdo; and 63 mph in Pierre, Reliance, and Miranda.  The strong winds diminished during the late afternoon hours of the 16th.

A period of northwest winds gusting to over 50 mph at times combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall of up to 2 inches to cause blizzard conditions from late morning to the start of the evening on January 16th. The blizzard caused widespread visibilities of less than one quarter mile in open areas. Travel was brought to a temporary halt and schools and some businesses were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to over 50 mph combined with existing snow cover and new snowfall of up to 2 inches to cause widespread frequent visibilities below a quarter mile in blowing snow. Schools and some businesses closed as travel temporarily became difficult to impossible.

Light freezing rain glazed the roads across parts of north central and northeast South Dakota during the mid afternoon hours. Due to slick roads, there was a two car and one semi accident six miles west of Mina on Highway 12 in eastern Edmunds County. The first car spun out on the ice and was hit by the semi. The second car swerved to avoid the crash but was still struck by the semi. Two people died along with one minor injury as a result of the accident.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong northwest winds were occasionally over 40 mph sustained, with gusts above 60 mph. A gust to 63 mph was recorded at Sioux Falls. An accident involving 20 vehicles on Interstate 90 near Humbolt was attributed on the high winds. The accident resulted in 5 injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta Clipper system generated light snow and strong winds across the region resulting in blizzard conditions. On Sunday morning, a band of moderate to heavy snow showers developed over North Dakota and swept down through our region producing cloud to ground lightning and thundersnow at times.  Snowfall amounts were generally three inches or less.  Wind gusts ranged from 45 to 55 mph at times.  Several no travel advisories were issued due to poor visibilities in blizzard conditions with state officials closing a large portion of Interstate-29 from Brookings to the North Dakota border.  With low visibilities near mile marker 178 on Interstate-29 near Watertown, a pickup struck the back of a car on Sunday afternoon of the 26th.  Two people were injured with a teenager dying a few days later as result of her injuries.

The coldest air in recent history moved into the region during the early morning hours of the 5th and continued into the afternoon hours of the 6th.  The combination of sub-zero temperatures with north winds produced dangerously cold wind chills from 40 below to around 55 degrees below zero.  Winds gusted to over 40 mph at times.  Several area activities were cancelled, as well as many schools on Monday the 6th.  Some of the coldest wind chills include; 56 below in Summit; 55 below near Hillhead; 54 below in Brandt and Webster; 53 below in Clear Lake and Frederick; 52 below in Herreid; 51 below in Clark and Leola; 50 below in Watertown, Sisseton, Bowdle, Hayti, Peever, Mahto, and McIntosh.

A low pressure system moved slowly from the central Rockies through the central Plains, producing snow and gusty winds across much of the area. Snowfall of one to two feet was reported across the northern Black Hills. Three to seven inches of snow were common across the plains of western and south central South Dakota, with a few locations across southwestern and south central South Dakota measuring as much as 12 inches of snow. Gusty northwest winds produced blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities less than a half mile at times.

Some of the coldest wind chills include; 56 below in Summit; 55 below near Hillhead; 54 below in Brandt and Webster; 53 below in Clear Lake and Frederick; 52 below in Herreid; 51 below in Clark and Leola; 50 below in Watertown, Sisseton, Bowdle, Hayti, Peever, Mahto, and McIntosh.

A strong Alberta Clipper system generated light snow and strong winds across the region resulting in blizzard conditions. On Sunday morning, a band of moderate to heavy snow showers developed over North Dakota and swept down through our region producing cloud to ground lightning and thundersnow at times.  Snowfall amounts were generally three inches or less.  Wind gusts ranged from 45 to 55 mph at times.  Several no travel advisories were issued due to poor visibilities in blizzard conditions with state officials closing a large portion of Interstate-29 from Brookings to the North Dakota border.  With low visibilities near mile marker 178 on Interstate-29 near Watertown, a pickup struck the back of a car on Sunday afternoon of the 26th.  Two people were injured with a teenager dying a few days later as result of her injuries.

A low pressure system moved slowly from the central Rockies through the central Plains, producing snow and gusty winds across much of the area. Snowfall of one to two feet was reported across the northern Black Hills. Three to seven inches of snow were common across the plains of western and south central South Dakota, with a few locations across southwestern and south central South Dakota measuring as much as 12 inches of snow. Gusty northwest winds produced blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities less than a half mile at times.

A clipper system passing across the region brought light snowfall and very strong northwest winds gusting in excess of 70 mph at times resulting in blizzard conditions.  The highest wind gusts include; 76 mph at the Brown County Landfill; 69 mph in Aberdeen and Cravens Corner; 52 mph near Webster; and 52 mph in Sisseton. Blizzard conditions ended during the late afternoon hours. A no travel advisory was issued in Grant, Codington, Hamlin, and Spink Counties due to poor visibilities.  The blizzard led to the cancellation of several area activities and schools and nearly impossible travel conditions.

The strong winds diminished during the late afternoon hours of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system passing across the region brought light snowfall and very strong northwest winds gusting in excess of 70 mph at times resulting in blizzard conditions.  The highest wind gusts include; 76 mph at the Brown County Landfill; 69 mph in Aberdeen and Cravens Corner; 52 mph near Webster; and 52 mph in Sisseton. Blizzard conditions ended during the late afternoon hours. A no travel advisory was issued in Grant, Codington, Hamlin, and Spink Counties due to poor visibilities.  The blizzard led to the cancellation of several area activities and schools and nearly impossible travel conditions.

A strong area of surface high pressure building into the region behind an Arctic cold front brought high winds to central and north central South Dakota during the early morning hours of the 16th.  Some of the strongest wind gusts include; 69 mph near Whitlocks Bay; 68 mph near Bullhead; 67 mph near Trail City; 66 mph in Foster Bay; 65 mph near Mellette and in Presho; 64 mph near Harrold and in Murdo; and 63 mph in Pierre, Reliance, and Miranda.  The strong winds diminished during the late afternoon hours of the 16th.

Freezing rain and freezing drizzle preceded snow in southeast South Dakota on December 15th. The freezing precipitation began during the predawn hours. Precipitation changed to snow during the day and the snow continued into the evening. Ice storm and winter storm conditions were produced in some areas, either for ice accumulations alone, or for the combination of ice and snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced ice accumulations estimated at a tenth of an inch, causing icy roads, as well as cancellations or early dismissals at some schools.  The freezing rain was followed by snowfall which was measured at 1.4 inches at Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area swinging southeast across the region brought heavy snow to parts of north central and northeast South Dakota. Six to nine inches of snow fell from the late evening of the 9th to the early evening of the 10th. Some snowfall amounts included; 6 inches at Aberdeen, Castlewood, Doland, Clear Lake, 11 miles northeast of Sisseton, south of Bristol, Bowdle and Mound City; 7 inches at Milbank, Clark, and 6 miles southeast of Mcintosh; 8 inches at Webster, Roscoe, Leola and Watertown, and 9 inches at Big Stone City.

A strong upper level disturbance and Arctic cold front moved through the area, bringing snow and much colder temperatures that persisted for several days. The heaviest snow fell over far northwestern into west central South Dakota, where four to nine inches of accumulation were reported. Gusty north winds produced areas of blowing snow across the plains.

Precipitation changed to snow during the day and the snow continued into the evening. Ice storm and winter storm conditions were produced in some areas, either for ice accumulations alone, or for the combination of ice and snow accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced ice accumulations, estimated at a couple tenths of an inch or more. The freezing rain was followed by 3 to 7 inches of snow, including 7.2 inches at Pickstown. Strong northwest winds produced drifting snow and areas of blowing snow. Roads were made very icy before becoming snow covered. There was damage to power lines and resulting power outages, and school classes were cancelled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt runoff along with ice jamming caused the Oak Creek near Wakpala to rise to above the flood stage of 10 feet during the late evening hours of the 10th. The creek reached 10.3 feet before falling back to below flood stage in the late evening hours of the 11th. One individual had to be evacuated from her home due to the flooding for a short time until the creek fell below flood stage.

Ice jamming and snowmelt caused the Grand River near Little Eagle to rise rapidly above the flood stage of 15 feet on the 11th in the morning hours.  The river rose quickly to near 22 feet by early afternoon before quickly falling back to below flood stage in the early evening hours.  The school in Little Eagle was dismissed early due to the flooding threat. Some ranchers also had to evacuate their livestock herds out of the river bottom due to the rapidly rising water.
A generally light snowfall produced accumulations of 3 to 5 inches in a few areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 5 inches in the southeast part of Charles Mix County, with 4.5 inches reported at Wagner.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 3 to 5.5 inches, with the 5.5 inch report at Volga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced penny to nickel size hail in Brookings County on the afternoon of March 31st.

Ice jamming and snowmelt caused the Moreau River near Whitehorse to rise to above the flood stage of 21 feet on the 11th during the afternoon hours. The river rose to over 27 feet on the 14th around noon before falling back to below flood stage on the 15th in the early afternoon. Lowland flooding occurred with some ranchers moving their cattle out of these areas.

Snowmelt runoff caused the Big Sioux River near Castlewood to rise to above the flood stage of 9 feet on the 14th in the early morning hours. The river rose to just over 10 feet before falling back to below flood stage on the 18th. Flooding of agricultural land resulted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple thunderstorms moving over the same region brought heavy rains of 2 to 5 inches to parts of northeast South Dakota. The heavy rains resulted in flash flooding across parts of the region. There was also a damaging tornado that moved across the northern edge of Lake Cochrane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on the north shore of Lake Cochrane and traveled up to an eighth of a mile to the northeast. The tornado caused minor damage overall and was mainly concentrated in an area along Edgewater drive at Lake Cochrane.  The damage was mainly to docks, boats, boat lifts, canopies, and shoreline shrubbery along with some minor tree damage. Two homes also suffered damage with one home having the western portion of the steel roof rolled back and tossed a small distance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over northeastern Wyoming interacted with a stationary boundary across western South Dakota and produced slow moving thunderstorms. With ample low level moisture over the area, the thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall. Between three and six inches of rain fell across portions of Harding and northwestern Butte Counties on June 17. The runoff from these storms and additional rain on June 18 and 19 caused significant flooding across much of northwestern South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred along Battle Creek, Fourmile Creek, Alkali Creek, Indian Creek, and the South Fork of the Moreau River. Several roads were flooded, including US Highway 85 between Belle Fourche and Redig, SD Highway 168, Harding Road, and Camp Crook Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along Bull Creek and Cherry Creek and its tributaries. Tankini and Red Scaffold Roads were flooded in a few locations. The highway was closed for eight days while temporary repairs were made and both lakes were drained to more manageable levels. The highway was closed again in early August for five weeks so permanent repairs could be made.

An upper level storm system brought monsoonal moisture over the northern high Plains, which combined with humid low-level southeasterly flow to produce heavy rain over portions of South Dakota. Flash flooding occurred in Rapid City and Rapid Valley. As much as 4.45 inches of rain was reported, with most of the rain falling within two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Box Elder Creek flooded Nemo Road northwest of Rapid City. Four to six inches of water covered numerous streets in Rapid City with as much as two feet of water in some locations and several basements were flooded. Some locations under water included underpasses on Haines Avenue and Maple Street at Interstate 90 and East Boulevard at the railroad tracks, portions of the Rushmore Mall parking lot, Deadwood Avenue near Commerce Road, and intersections along Fifth Street.

June flooding on the James River near Columbia continued into early July before falling back to below flood stage in the middle of July.  Flood stage at Columbia is 13 feet.  The river rose to 14.2 feet in late June.  The James River near Stratford also rose to just above flood stage in early July before falling back to below flood stage in mid July. The river rose to just above the flood stage of 14 feet to 14.1 feet on July 12th. Some flooding of agricultural land along with a few roads occurred.

A supercell thunderstorm developed over western Shannon County and tracked southeastward into Nebraska. The storm produced very large hail in the Oglala area with a few hailstones as large as softballs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail, with a few hailstones baseball to softball size, caused property damage in Oglala.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought rain to much of the area and some early season snow to the Black Hills area. Rain changed to snow during the overnight hours. The heaviest snow fell at elevations above 5000 feet in the Black Hills west of Highway 385 from Custer to northwest of Lead, where four to eight inches of snow was reported. Light snow also fell over the northern and eastern foothills, including Rapid City, which was the earliest measurable snowfall on record.

The heaviest snow fell at elevations above 5000 feet in the Black Hills west of Highway 385 from Custer to northwest of Lead, where four to eight inches of snow was reported. Light snow also fell over the northern and eastern foothills, including Rapid City, which was the earliest measurable snowfall on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pactola Dam observer measured 5.8 inches of snow, which broke the record for earliest measurable snowfall. The previous record 2.0 inches on September 16, 1965.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced quarter size hail and wind gusts over 60 mph near Trail City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty-five mph winds were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient over the Northern Plains produced gusty winds across the area. The strongest winds were over northwestern South Dakota, where some gusts around 60 mph were recorded during the late morning and early afternoon.

Thunderstorms produced penny to quarter size hail northwest of Veblen and near Sisseton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down quickly northwest of Long Lake with no damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm became severe over central Todd County and tracked southeastward into Nebraska. The storm produced hail to the size of ping pong balls in the Mission area.

Arctic air combined with strong northwest winds to bring bitter cold wind chills to central and northeast South Dakota east of the Missouri River. Bitter wind chills of 35 below to around 40 below occurred. Some of the coldest wind chills include, 39 degrees below zero west of Long Lake; 40 degrees below zero at Highmore; 41 degrees below zero near Roy Lake; and 42 degrees below zero at Summit.

A low pressure system brought snow to much of western South Dakota. Precipitation started as rain during the evening of March 10 and switched to snow during the night. Snow continued through the morning and decreased by midday. The heaviest snow occurred over the northern Black Hills and northern foothills, where upslope enhanced snow developed. Four to eight inches of snow was reported in this area, with locally higher amounts.

A low pressure system passed over the Northern Plains, bringing rain and snow to much of western South Dakota. Rain quickly changed to snow over the northern Black Hills area during the evening, with occasionally heavy snow during the night. Upslope-enhanced snow developed as northerly winds increased. Six to 12 inches of snow was reported across much of the northern Black Hills and foothills from Spearfish to Piedmont. Gusty winds also produced some blowing and drifting snow, with greatly reduced visibilities at times.

A strong surface low pressure area moving across the region brought mixed precipitation to all of the area including rain, freezing rain, sleet, snow along with some thunder.  As the precipitation changed to over all snow, northwest winds increased substantially to 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 to 60 mph causing widespread blizzard conditions.  Much of the area received a coating of ice with trace amounts up to a tenth of an inch with several locations receiving up to 2 inches of sleet. Snowfall amounts from 2 to as much as 10 inches occurred with this storm. The precipitation changed over to snow in the morning out west and into the late afternoon hours across the east. The light snow did not end in the far east until the early morning hours of April 1st. |  |Many schools, government offices, and businesses were closed or cancelled early. Travel was not advised across much of region with Interstate-29 being closed for a time from Brookings to the North Dakota border. Some snowfall amounts included, 3 inches at Aberdeen; 4 inches at Roscoe and Mobridge; 5 inches at Kennebec, near Hayes, and Pollock; 6 inches at Pierre; 7 inches at Bowdle; and 10 inches at Sisseton.  Some of the highest wind gusts included, 55 mph at Watertown; 59 mph at Sisseton; and 62 mph near Eureka.

Warm temperatures caused rapid snowmelt and ice break-up along area rivers. Backwater from ice jams caused minor flooding on the Little Missouri, Moreau, and White Rivers in northwest and south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the White River from north of Hamill to the Missouri River. Flooding was confined to agricultural land along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Northern Plains; bringing rain, snow and strong winds to the region. Precipitation started as rain before colder air pushed in and changed precipitation to snow during the night. As rain changed over to snow, north to northwest winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph by the morning. The heaviest snow fell over the northern Black Hills and northwest South Dakota, where five to ten inches was reported with locally higher amounts across far northwestern South Dakota. The combination of snow and wind caused blizzard conditions over much of the western South Dakota plains during the morning and early afternoon. Interstate 90 was closed for a brief time around midday between Box Elder and Wall due to numerous accidents.

A surface low pressure area moving off to the east brought some heavy snow into far northeast South Dakota. A narrow band of heavy snow set up across this area bringing from 6 to 11 inches of snow. Snowfall amounts included, 6 inches in Estelline; 8 inches near Revillo; and 11 inches in Clear Lake. Snowfall came down at a rate of 1 to 2 inches per hour for several hours with visibility near zero at times. Travel became difficult, if not, impossible with many cars ending up in the ditch.

A strong low pressure system moved across the Northern Plains; bringing rain, snow and strong winds to the region. Precipitation started as rain before colder air pushed in and changed precipitation to snow during the night. As rain changed over to snow, north to northwest winds increased to 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph by the morning. The heaviest snow fell over the northern Black Hills and northwest South Dakota, where five to ten inches was reported with locally higher amounts across far northwestern South Dakota. The combination of snow and wind caused blizzard conditions over much of the western South Dakota plains during the morning and early afternoon. Interstate 90 was closed for a brief time around midday between Box Elder and Wall due to numerous accidents.

A strong surface low pressure area moving across the region brought mixed precipitation to all of the area including rain, freezing rain, sleet, snow along with some thunder.  As the precipitation changed to over all snow, northwest winds increased substantially to 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 to 60 mph causing widespread blizzard conditions.  Much of the area received a coating of ice with trace amounts up to a tenth of an inch with several locations receiving up to 2 inches of sleet. Snowfall amounts from 2 to as much as 10 inches occurred with this storm. The precipitation changed over to snow in the morning out west and into the late afternoon hours across the east. The light snow did not end in the far east until the early morning hours of April 1st. |  |Many schools, government offices, and businesses were closed or cancelled early. Travel was not advised across much of region with Interstate-29 being closed for a time from Brookings to the North Dakota border. Some snowfall amounts included, 3 inches at Aberdeen; 4 inches at Roscoe and Mobridge; 5 inches at Kennebec, near Hayes, and Pollock; 6 inches at Pierre; 7 inches at Bowdle; and 10 inches at Sisseton.  Some of the highest wind gusts included, 55 mph at Watertown; 59 mph at Sisseton; and 62 mph near Eureka.

A potent surface low pressure area tracking across northern Minnesota along with a strong cold front dropping across our region brought very strong northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph.  The very strong winds along with the existing snow cover brought widespread blizzard conditions across northeast South Dakota resulting in significant travel problems.  A school bus along with a few cars were stuck in the ditch.

Snowfall became heavy in a narrow band extending from Pickstown northeast to just west of Mitchell and just west of Brookings. The snow fell during the late morning and afternoon of March 18th. Accumulations varied from 4 to 10 inches along the axis of this band. The snowfall was accompanied by northerly winds gusting occasionally to around 30 mph. These winds caused some areas of blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 2 to 5 inches, with 4.3 inches reported near Crooks. Winds gusting at times to near 30 mph caused a few areas of blowing snow.

A surface low pressure area moving off to the east brought some heavy snow into far northeast South Dakota. A narrow band of heavy snow set up across this area bringing from 6 to 11 inches of snow. Snowfall amounts included, 6 inches in Estelline; 8 inches near Revillo; and 11 inches in Clear Lake. Snowfall came down at a rate of 1 to 2 inches per hour for several hours with visibility near zero at times. Travel became difficult, if not, impossible with many cars ending up in the ditch.

Arctic air combined with strong northwest winds to bring bitter cold wind chills to central and northeast South Dakota east of the Missouri River. Bitter wind chills of 35 below to around 40 below occurred. Some of the coldest wind chills include, 39 degrees below zero west of Long Lake; 40 degrees below zero at Highmore; 41 degrees below zero near Roy Lake; and 42 degrees below zero at Summit.

Bitter wind chills of 35 below to around 40 below occurred. Snowfall became heavy in a narrow band extending from Pickstown northeast to just west of Mitchell and just west of Brookings. The snow fell during the late morning and afternoon of March 18th. Accumulations varied from 4 to 10 inches along the axis of this band. The snowfall was accompanied by northerly winds gusting occasionally to around 30 mph. These winds caused some areas of blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 9 inches in a narrow band over the central part of the county, with 9 inches reported at Lake Andes.  Winds gusting at times to near 30 mph caused a few areas of blowing and drifting snow. Some schools were dismissed early. Less than 2 inches fell near the northwest and southeast ends  of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent surface low pressure area tracking across northern Minnesota along with a strong cold front dropping across our region brought very strong northwest winds of 30 to 45 mph.  The very strong winds along with the existing snow cover brought widespread blizzard conditions across northeast South Dakota resulting in significant travel problems.  A school bus along with a few cars were stuck in the ditch.

Warm temperatures caused rapid snowmelt and ice break-up along area rivers. Backwater from ice jams caused minor flooding on the Little Missouri, Moreau, and White Rivers in northwest and south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Little Missouri River due to ice jams. The river gauge at Camp Crook crested at 16.2 feet, or 4.2 feet above flood stage. Outbuildings and agricultural land in the area were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures caused rapid snowmelt and ice break-up along area rivers. Backwater from ice jams caused minor flooding on the Little Missouri, Moreau, and White Rivers in northwest and south central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor ice jam flooding occurred along the Moreau River. The river gauge along Highway 73 northwest of Faith crested at 17.4 feet, or 1.4 feet above flood stage. Some agricultural land was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area moving across the region brought mixed precipitation to all of the area including rain, freezing rain, sleet, snow along with some thunder.  As the precipitation changed to over all snow, northwest winds increased substantially to 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 to 60 mph causing widespread blizzard conditions.  Much of the area received a coating of ice with trace amounts up to a tenth of an inch with several locations receiving up to 2 inches of sleet. Snowfall amounts from 2 to as much as 10 inches occurred with this storm. The precipitation changed over to snow in the morning out west and into the late afternoon hours across the east. The light snow did not end in the far east until the early morning hours of April 1st. |  |Many schools, government offices, and businesses were closed or cancelled early. Travel was not advised across much of region with Interstate-29 being closed for a time from Brookings to the North Dakota border. Some snowfall amounts included, 3 inches at Aberdeen; 4 inches at Roscoe and Mobridge; 5 inches at Kennebec, near Hayes, and Pollock; 6 inches at Pierre; 7 inches at Bowdle; and 10 inches at Sisseton.  Some of the highest wind gusts included, 55 mph at Watertown; 59 mph at Sisseton; and 62 mph near Eureka.

Heavy snow on the roads along with visibility reduction contributed to a fatal accident 20 miles west of Watertown on U.S. Highway 212. The driver was eastbound on the wrong side of the road (westbound lane) when he approached a westbound snowplow.  He swerved to miss the snowplow and lost control of his vehicle. As his vehicle was sliding sideways in the westbound lane, it was struck by another vehicle with two people inside. The driver in the wrong lane was killed with the other two people sustaining serious, but non-life threatening injuries.

Thunderstorms produced numerous events of large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding, and areal flooding across most of southeast South Dakota from late morning through the evening of June 16th. The areal flooding was a longer term event and persisted through much of June 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rain caused flooding of fields and other lowlands, including several roads, homes, and businesses. This flooding lasted for almost two days, and was aggravated locally by additional storms during that time. Some roads were damaged or washed out.

Repeating heavy rain caused record flooding of the lower Big Sioux River from June 15th through June 26th. Considerable flooding of farmland and other lowlands included numerous roads, with some roads damaged and a few washed out. A few farm homes and other buildings were damaged by floodwaters. There was also considerable damage to fencing on farms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repeated heavy rains caused record flooding of the Big Sioux River. Farmland and other lowlands including numerous roads were flooded. Some roads were damaged, and some secondary roads were washed out. A few farm homes, other farm buildings, and some fences were damaged by floodwaters. The river crested at a record 12.92 feet above flood stage at Hawarden, Iowa on June 17th. Interstate 29 was temporarily closed along a 22 mile stretch to facilitate levee building to divert some Big Sioux River water to McCook Lake, but the Interstate was re-opened when the river crested at a lower level than was feared. A levee breach across the river from Akron, Iowa, briefly aggravated lowland flooding before it was repaired.

Large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding, along with several tornadoes occurred with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph winds were estimated by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of 3 to 4 inches had fallen across parts of southern Lyman County resulting in the flooding of several roads mainly between Interstate-90 and the White River south of Kennebec. No travel was advised on a road two miles south of Kennebec.
A slow-moving supercell thunderstorm developed over far northern Perkins County and tracked eastward. The storm produced hail to golf ball size across the area.

Thunderstorms produced large hail and heavy rain, which caused flash flooding, at a few locations in southeast South Dakota during the late afternoon and evening of June 14th. The heavy rain also caused longer term lowland flooding of fields and other low areas including roads, that lasted well into the daytime hours of June 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rain caused flooding of some fields and other lowlands, including several roads. This flooding lasted well into the daytime hours after the rain subsided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm became severe as it moved across the central Black Hills. Large hail and strong wind gusts were reported from Harney Peak and Mount Rushmore to Custer State Park and the foothills southeast of the Black Hills.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated near 60 mph.

Numerous thunderstorms developed along a stationary boundary across central and north central South Dakota. Several of these thunderstorms became severe with large hail up to baseball size, damaging winds, flash flooding, along with an isolated tornado.
A severe thunderstorm developed over the central Black Hills and moved over the Rapid City area, dropping large hail from southwest of town almost to Wasta.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inch diameter tree branches were downed by the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts accompanied a thunderstorm that moved across the Creighton area of northeastern Pennington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter estimated wind gusts near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nickel hail fell from a thunderstorm in northeast Corson County.
Severe thunderstorms brought large hail and very strong winds to parts of north central and northeast South Dakota.

Thunderstorms during the evening of June 18th produced tornadoes in Jerauld County, with one tornado crossing briefly into Beadle County. The tornadoes included one that caused severe damage to Wessington Springs. There was also a report of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado damaged at least 43 homes in Wessington Springs, making at least 26 of them uninhabitable. The tornado also destroyed at least 3 businesses and damaged 9 others. The tornado also caused considerable damage to power lines, power poles, and trees, resulting in power outages to the entire town. Vehicles and signs were also damaged. The damage included an estimated 1.2 million dollars in damage to public infrastructure. There were no reported fatalities and one minor injury. The tornado also caused an unknown amount of damage to corn and soybean crops.
